records.ety: [OE. crois, croys, cros; the former fr. OF. crois, croiz, F. croix, fr. L. crux; the second is perh. directly fr. Prov. cros, crotz. fr. the same L. crux; cf. Icel. kross. Cf. Crucial, Crusade, Cruise, Crux.]

defs: A gibbet, consisting of two pieces of timber placed transversely upon one another, in various forms, as a T, or +, with the horizontal piece below the upper end of the upright, or as an X. It was anciently used in the execution of criminals.
quotes: Nailed to the cross By his own nation.

defs: The sign or mark of the cross, made with the finger, or in ink, etc., or actually represented in some material; the symbol of Christ's death; the ensign and chosen symbol of Christianity, of a Christian people, and of Christendom.
quotes: The custom of making the sign of the cross with the hand or finger, as a means of conferring blessing or preserving from evil, is very old.
Before the cross has waned the crescent's ray.
Tis where the cross is preached.

defs: Affliction regarded as a test of patience or virtue; trial; disappointment; opposition; misfortune.
quotes: Heaven prepares a good man with crosses.

defs: A piece of money stamped with the figure of a cross, also, that side of such a piece on which the cross is stamped; hence, money in general.
quotes: I should bear no cross if I did bear you; for I think you have no money in your purse.

defs: An appendage or ornament or anything in the form of a cross; a badge or ornamental device of the general shape of a cross; hence, such an ornament, even when varying considerably from that form; thus, the Cross of the British Order of St. George and St. Michael consists of a central medallion with seven arms radiating from it.
A monument in the form of a cross, or surmounted by a cross, set up in a public place; as, a market cross; a boundary cross; Charing Cross in London.
quotes: Dun-Edin's Cross, a pillared stone, Rose on a turret octagon.

defs: A common heraldic bearing, of which there are many varieties. See the Illustration, above.
The crosslike mark or symbol used instead of a signature by those unable to write.
quotes: Five Kentish abbesses . . . .subscribed their names and crosses.

defs: Church lands.
A line drawn across or through another line.
A mixing of breeds or stock, especially in cattle breeding; or the product of such intermixture; a hybrid of any kind.
quotes: Toning down the ancient Viking into a sort of a cross between Paul Jones and Jeremy Diddler.

defs: An instrument for laying of offsets perpendicular to the main course.
A pipe-fitting with four branches the axes of which usually form's right angle.
Not parallel; lying or falling athwart; transverse; oblique; intersecting.
quotes: The cross refraction of the second prism.

defs: Not accordant with what is wished or expected; interrupting; adverse; contrary; thwarting; perverse.
quotes: The cross and unlucky issue of my design.
The article of the resurrection seems to lie marvelously cross to the common experience of mankind.
We are both love's captives, but with fates so cross, One must be happy by the other's loss.

defs: Characterized by, or in a state of, peevishness, fretfulness, or ill humor; as, a cross man or woman.
quotes: He had received a cross answer from his mistress.

defs: Made in an opposite direction, or an inverse relation; mutually inverse; interchanged; as, cross interrogatories; cross marriages, as when a brother and sister marry persons standing in the same relation to each other.
Athwart; across.
quotes: A fox was taking a walk one night cross a village.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Crossed (krst; 115); p. pr. & vb. n. Crossing.]

defs: To put across or athwart; to cause to intersect; as, to cross the arms.
To lay or draw something, as a line, across; as, to cross the letter t.
To pass from one side to the other of; to pass or move over; to traverse; as, to cross a stream.
quotes: A hunted hare . . . crosses and confounds her former track.

defs: To pass, as objects going in an opposite direction at the same time.
To run counter to; to thwart; to obstruct; to hinder; to clash or interfere with.
quotes: In each thing give him way; cross him in nothing.
An oyster may be crossed in love.

defs: To interfere and cut off; to debar.
quotes: To cross me from the golden time I look for.

defs: To make the sign of the cross upon; -- followed by the reflexive pronoun; as, he crossed himself.
To cancel by marking crosses on or over, or drawing a line across; to erase; -- usually with out, off, or over; as, to cross out a name.
To cause to interbreed; -- said of different stocks or races; to mix the breed of.
To lie or be athwart.
To move or pass from one side to the other, or from place to place; to make a transit; as, to cross from New York to Liverpool.
To be inconsistent.
quotes: Men's actions do not always cross with reason.

defs: To interbreed, as races; to mix distinct breeds.
quotes: If two individuals of distinct races cross, a third is invariably produced different from either.
